Introduction
a b s t r a c t
Purpose: Safe Routes to School (SRTS) was a federally funded transportation program for facilitating
physically active commuting to and from school in children through improvements of the built environment. There is evidence that SRTS programs increase walking and bicycling in school-age children
but their impact on pedestrian and bicyclist safety has not been adequately examined. We investigate the
impact and effects of the SRTS program on school-age pedestrian and bicyclist injuries in a nationwide
sample in the United States.
Methods: Data were crash records for school-age children (5e19 years) and adults (30e64 years)
U.S. states for a 16-year period (1995e2010). Multilevel negative binomial models were used to examine
the association between SRTS intervention and the risk of pedestrian and bicyclist injury in children aged
5e19 years.
Results: SRTS was associated with an approximately 23% reduction (incidence rate ratio ¼ 0.77
confidence interval ¼ 0.65e0.92) in pedestrian/bicyclist injury risk and a 20% reduction in pedestrian/
bicyclist fatality risk (incidence rate ratio ¼ 0.80
children compared to adults aged 30e64 years.
Conclusions: Implementation of the SRTS program appears to have contributed to improving traffic safety
for school-age children in the United States.

Methods
Individual-level pedestrian and bicyclist injury data for a
16-year period (1995e2010) were obtained from the U.S. Department of Transportation National Highway and Traffic Administration State Data System [22] for 18 states: Arkansas
Connecticut

program to encourage both forms of active travel. Data on SRTS
funding allocations were obtained from the National Center for Safe
Routes to School [23]. Data on the number of roadway miles in each
state were obtained from the U.S. Department of Transportation

roadways were from 2008 and contained variables on types of
roads categorized by rural versus urban. Population data were obtained from the U.S. Census [25].

Discussion
The decline in traffic-related injuries in the United States is a
public health success story [29]. Drivers and motor vehicle occupants have accrued the most benefit. The challenge remains to
enhance the safety of the most vulnerable roadway users
children and older adults
infrastructure [29]. There is also concern that some of the decline in
pediatric pedestrian risk may have come at the expense of healthy
active behaviors [8

travel and in addressing parents’ concerns about their children’s
safety getting to and from school [12

trend represented by the reduction in adult injuries and restricting
to school-travel hours
column2: we found evidence that SRTS was associated

column1: with a 14% to 16% decline in pedestrian and bicyclist injury risk and
a 13% decline in pedestrian and bicyclist fatality risk in 18 U.S. states.
This finding is consistent with the experience of NYC [19] and the
state of Texas [21]. The safety benefit of SRTS programs reported in
this study is likely conservative because the incidence rates based
on population data do not take into account the increased exposure
to walking and bicycling associated with SRTS programs.

**3. Methods**
_3.1. Background_
This study uses data from a three-year natural experiment study
(2012–2015) of the Comox-Helmcken Greenway (“Comox Greenway”)
in Vancouver. This paper builds on recently published work where GHG
emissions reductions (Ngo et al.
& sedentary behavior reductions (Frank et al.
the Comox Greenway were documented. Urban greenways are land­
scaped and traffic-calmed pathways with a mix of bicycle facilities and
other streetscape improvements that link open spaces
facilities
column2: and neighborhood centers together. Greenways support a va­

column1: riety of active travel uses

assesses the primary aim of the greenway—to promote cycling. For the
present study
column2: we ask the following questions: 1) is the construction of

column1: the Comox Greenway associated with changes in cycling trip frequency
for nearby residents? and 2) are carshare members more likely to use a
bicycle compared to their non-carshare counterparts?
_3.2. Study area_
The Comox Greenway is a major active transportation corridor
extending east-to-west through downtown Vancouver. It is located in
Vancouver’s West End neighborhood
and commercial area. The 2-km long route was retrofitted with a mix of
cycling facilities and other streetscape improvements to improve the
conditions for people of all ages and abilities to walk and bike. New
cycling facilities included one-way shared on-street with counterflow
lanes (22% of the route in terms of length)
and two-way shared on-street (49%). These changes were intended to
reduce the amount of motor vehicle traffic
for vulnerable road users
column2: and reduce traffic stress for pedestrians and

column1: cyclists using the route. Fig. 1 illustrates the streetscape changes before
and after the greenway retrofit along the corridor. In addition to the
reallocation of road space from motor vehicles to active modes
(in order from top to bottom) included the restriction of motor vehicles
to one-way travel only; intersection upgrades including signalization
and bicycle pavement markings to facilitate safer crossings; street
furniture such as seating to facilitate social connections; and new green
space and landscaping.

**5. Discussion**
This paper provides a causal assessment of the effect of an urban
greenway retrofit on cycling trip frequency in a dense
neighborhood. For residents living nearby within 300 m of the ComoxHelmcken Greenway
column2: there was a positive effect of the greenway

column1: through an increase in cycling trips. The zero-inflated negative binomial
model showed that the opening of the greenway resulted in a 252%
increase in cycling trips for residents within 300 m relative to those
further away. The model indicated that on average

age cohorts.

during the baseline period. While the coefficient was in the same di­
rection during the follow-up period (an increase in the odds by 43%)

**6. Conclusions**
This study offers timely causal evidence in supporting the realloca­
tion of road space in dense urban settings for active transportation and
health promoting purposes in response to the ongoing COVID-19
pandemic. This study finds a positive impact of a new urban greenway
on cycling for residents living within 300 m of an urban greenway
compared to those living further away. Our study provides causal evi­
dence on the effect of urban infrastructure investment on travel behavior
change by employing a unique experimental-and-control pre-post
research design. This is not possible through more traditional crosssectional research designs. The results suggest that greenways could
be an effective strategy to boost urban cycling and can even overcome
other programmatic and policy changes such as carsharing that can
make driving relatively easier than active modes in highly urban set­
tings. The evidence generated from this study support a tangible shift in
policy as leaders from many cities around the world strive to make
needed investments to achieve a radical transition to more sustainable
forms of urban transportation.

to quantify the health risk and benefits of car trips substitution by bikes trips from European BSS. The estimated health

# Timing and effect of a safe routes to school program on child pedestrian injury risk during school travel hours: Bayesian changepoint and difference-in-differences analysis
Charles DiMaggio [1*]
column3: Peter A Muennig [3] and Guohua Li [1]

column1: Abstract
Background: In 2005
column2: the US Congress allocated $612 million for a national Safe Routes to School (SRTS) program

column1: to encourage walking and bicycling to schools. We evaluated the effectiveness of a SRTS in controlling pedestrian
injuries among school-age children.
Methods: Bayesian changepoint analysis was applied to model the quarterly counts of pedestrian injuries among 5- to
19-year old children in New York City between 2001 and 2010 during school-travel hours in census tracts with and without
SRTS. Overdispersed Poisson model was used to estimate difference-in-differences in injury risk between census tracts with
and without SRTS following the changepoint.
Results: In SRTS-intervention census tracts
column2: a change point in the quarterly counts of injuries was identified in the second

column1: quarter of 2008
column2: which was consistent with the timing of the implementation of SRTS interventions. In census tracts with

column1: SRTS interventions
column2: the estimated quarterly rates of pedestrian injury per 10

column1: during school-travel hours were 3.47 (95% Credible Interval [CrI] 2.67

column2: 1.50) after the changepoint. There was no change in the average number of quarterly injuries in non-SRTS census

column1: tracts. Overdispersed Poisson modeling revealed that SRTS implementation was associated with a 44% reduction (95%
Confidence Interval [CI] 87% decrease to 130% increase) in school-age pedestrian injury risk during school-travel hours.
Conclusions: Bayesian changepoint analysis of quarterly counts of school-age pedestrian injuries successfully identified
the timing of SRTS intervention in New York City. Implementation of the SRTS program in New York City appears to be
effective in reducing school-age pedestrian injuries during school-travel hours.
Keywords: Pediatric; Pedestrian; Injury; School travel; Changepoint; Bayesian

**Abstract**
**Background:** Safe Routes to School (SRTS) is a federally funded transportation program for facilitating physically
active commuting to and from school in children through improvements of the built environment
bicycle lanes
column2: and safe crossings. Although it is evident that SRTS programs increase walking and bicycling in

column1: school-age children
column2: their impact on pedestrian and bicyclist injury has not been adequately examined.

column1: **Methods:** We analyzed quarterly traffic crash data between January 2008 and June 2013 in Texas to assess the effect
of the SRTS program implemented after 2009 on school-age pedestrian and bicyclist injuries.
**Results:** The annualized rates of pedestrian and bicyclist injuries between pre- and post-SRTS periods declined
42.5% (95% confidence interval (CI) 39.6% to 45.4%) in children aged 5 to 19 years and 33.0% (95% CI 30.5% to 35.5%)
in adults aged 30 to 64 years. Negative binomial modeling revealed that SRTS intervention was associated with a 14%
reduction in the school-age pedestrian and bicyclist injury incidence rate ratio (IRR 0.86
effect of the SRTS intervention on pedestrian and bicyclist fatalities was similar though smaller in magnitude and was
not statistically significant (adjusted IRR 0.90
**Conclusions:** These results indicate that the implementation of the SRTS program in Texas may have contributed to
declines in school-age pedestrian and bicyclist injuries.

**ABSTRACT**
In the last decade
column2: there has been an increased focus in California on encouraging children to

column1: walk and bicycle to school safely. In 1999
column2: the California Legislature created the Safe Routes to

column1: School (SR2S) program
column2: authorizing issuance of a competitive grant process for roadway

column1: construction projects. There has been an overall decline in the numbers of child
pedestrian/bicyclist collisions in California as a whole. When compared with the control areas
the SR2S project areas did not show a greater decline in numbers of collisions. However
likely that the number of children walking/bicycling in the SR2S project areas increased over the
relevant time frame. When changes in mobility in the program areas are taken into account
SR2S program appears to be associated with a net safety benefit for affected school age students.

**INTRODUCTION**

column2: close to 50% of American children walked to school. Today

column1: children who live within one mile of school
column2: the proportion declined from 87% to 31% in that

column1: same time period (1). Similar trends have been found in many other Western countries
among diverse populations and settings. (2). Walking to school has primarily been replaced by
transport in private vehicles driven by a parent. Martin _et al_ (3) notes that distance and traffic are
the primary reasons why parents do not let their children walk/bike to school. Also
study by Fischbeck and Huey (4) found that bicycling and walking have the second and third
highest fatality rate per trip among other types of school transport.
The California Legislature created the Safe Routes to School (SR2S) program in 1999 as
a response to two concerns: the decline in numbers of children walking/bicycling to school
the potential risk of death or injury for those who do walk/bike. This landmark legislation
authorized issuance of a competitive grant process for roadway construction projects. The
legislation had two goals: to reduce child injuries and fatalities near schools and to increase
walking and bicycling activity among students at elementary
column2: middle and high schools. Since its

column1: inception in 2000
column2: the SR2S program has funded 570 projects with a total cost of over $190

column1: million.
This paper presents results of a study that estimated changes in pedestrian and cyclist
safety that may be attributable to the SR2S program. The study is based on the evaluation of a
representative sample of 125 of the 570 projects that received SR2S funding in the first three
years of the program. The study complements previous research that assessed the impact of the
SR2S program on rates of walking and bicycling to school. (5)
In this paper
column2: we first describe the methods used for gathering and collating data. We then

column1: explain the methodology used to characterize the 125 SR2S projects that comprised our study
sample. The effects on walking and bicycling are reported
column2: followed by a quantitative and a

column1: qualitative investigation of changes in child pedestrian/bicyclist crash occurrence.
**CALIFORNIA SAFE ROUTES TO SCHOOLS PROGRAM**

column2: the Safe Routes to School (SR2S) highway construction program was created by the

column1: California State Legislature through Assembly Bill 1475. The bill provided for federal
transportation funds to be used for the construction of bicycle and pedestrian safety and traffic
calming projects. Five basic types of infrastructure improvements were allowed: sidewalk
installation and upgrading
column2: traffic calming and speed reduction measures

column1: signals
column3: and construction of bicycle paths or other

column1: bicycle facilities. In the first cycle
column2: funding was limited to engineering improvements; however

column1: subsequent application cycles allowed funds to be used for education and traffic safety
awareness programs to support the infrastructure changes.
The legislation that created the SR2S program also mandated that the California
Department of Transportation study its effectiveness. Among other outcomes
was to focus on the effectiveness of the program in reducing crashes
involving children in the vicinity of the projects. The University of California Traffic Safety
Center (TSC) was contracted by Caltrans to conduct the evaluation
column2: and a report to the California

column1: Legislature was submitted in early 2007.

**Statistical analysis**
Rates were calculated as injury counts per unit of time. Post-intervention rates were compared

**RESULTS**
**Characteristics of SR2S projects**
Most projects—well over half—included some type of sidewalk upgrade as part of the
improvements. Upgrading intersection crossings was also a high priority for applicants
more than one-third of projects including this component. Most projects included multiple
improvement types. The greatest proportion of schools affected by SR2S projects were
elementary schools.

**CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS**
The Safe Routes to School program has captured the attention of traffic engineers
advocates
column3: communities and families. Anecdotally it has been a resounding success.

column1: Through the quantitative and qualitative analyses conducted as part of the legislative mandate
the SR2S program appears to have been effective in achieving its goals of increasing
walking/bicycling and improving safety.
A significant challenge is the lack of data for evaluating the success of individual projects.
Future assessments of changes in traffic data can be improved through obtaining reliable
quantifiable estimates of the change from before the SR2S construction and after. This
specificity of data will also help in the identification of the types of projects that result in the
greatest safety increases and the populations that most greatly benefit.
